overview.description: A table to display data in rows and columns with advanced features like sorting, pagination, row selection, and expandable rows.
overview.purpose: A table to display data in rows and columns with advanced features like sorting, pagination, row selection, and expandable rows.
whenToUse: When you need to present tabular data with advanced interaction capabilities.
whenNotToUse: For simple lists or when a grid layout is more appropriate.

examples.basic: <mc-table data="[{"id":1,"name":"Madrid Maersk","type":"Container ship","built":"2017","length":399,"capacity":19630,"inService":true,"status":"On schedule","speed":"16.2","position":"Track on map","lastPort":"Shanghai","lastCountry":"China","lastUpdate":"09/12/2025"},{"id":2,"name":"Mary Maersk","type":"Container ship","built":"2013","length":399,"capacity":18270,"inService":true,"status":"Delayed","speed":"2.1","position":"Track on map","lastPort":"Busan","lastCountry":"South Korea","lastUpdate":"10/12/2025"}]" columns="[{"id":"name","label":"Name","noWrap":true,"sticky":false},{"id":"inService","label":"In Service"}]" datakey="id" headerstickyoffsetselector="" caption="A caption to describe the table below" fit="medium" outerborderstyle="solid" verticallinestyle="none" horizontallinestyle="solid" verticalalign="middle" height="auto" stateslotheight="auto" customstyles="" hidecolumns="[]" sortdefaultcolumnid="capacity" sortdefaultdirection="ascending" pagination="none" paginationmode="static" paginationtotalrecords="200" paginationarialabel="Navigate Table Rows" paginationcurrentpage="1" paginationpagesize="10" paginationpagesizeoptions="[10,20]" paginationalignment="center" selectalllabel="Select all rows" selectlabel="Select row" selected="[]" selectrowdisabled="[]" expandopened="[]" expandpadding="default" expandtriggerlabel="Expand row"></mc-table>

props.name: data
props.description: An array of objects containing the data to be rendered by the table

props.name: columns
props.description: Find a `columns` API detail description in the Columns Documentation story. An array of TableColumn objects informing the table how to render columns and it's data cells. If no columns are passed, the table will infer columns from the first row of data.

props.name: datakey
props.description:
The unique data key field name.

If not supplied the table will create a key field called "id"

props.name: labels
props.description: Custom labels for mc-table component. By default labels are loaded in EN language. Check types documentation and the examples stories to see how to use custom labels property.

props.name: headerhidden
props.description: Hide the table header

props.name: headersticky
props.description: Make the header row "sticky"

props.name: headerstickyviewport
props.description: Attach the sticky header to the viewport instead of an inner scroll container.

props.name: headerstickyoffsetselector
props.description: CSS selector of an element whose height should be used as the sticky header offset. The table listens for size changes on that element.

props.name: footer
props.description: Show a table footer.

props.name: footersticky
props.description: Make the footer row "sticky"

props.name: caption
props.defaultValue: A caption to describe the table below
props.description:
The caption to describe the table can be passed as a property or as a slot `
This is a caption
`

props.name: fit
props.description: Description missing

props.name: zebrastripes
props.description: Zebra stripes

props.name: outerborderstyle
props.description: Style applied to outer border.

props.name: verticallinestyle
props.description: Border style applied to vertical lines.

props.name: horizontallinestyle
props.description: Border style applied to horizontal lines.

props.name: disablerowhighlightonhover
props.description: Disable highlight a row on mouse hover

props.name: nowrap
props.description: Enforces a default "nowrap" on cells

props.name: verticalalign
props.description: Vertical alignment of cells

props.name: height
props.description:
Height

 The width is determined by the parent container.

props.name: disableoverflow
props.description: Disables the default overflow "hidden" of the table

props.name: stateslotheight
props.description: Height of state slot in the table body

props.name: customstyles
props.description: Custom CSS styles that can be used to customize table rows etc.

props.name: disableroundedcorners
props.description: Disables rounded corners

props.name: disableplaceholderfooters
props.description: By default table will render "placeholder" footers for the select and expand columns. You can disable them by setting the "disableplaceholderfooters" attribute to true.

props.name: truncate
props.description: Column-level property: Set `truncate: true` on individual columns to truncate cell text with ellipsis. A tooltip will automatically display the full text on hover or focus. Look up story example for more details.

props.name: hidecolumns
props.description: Column Ids to hide.

props.name: sortdefaultcolumnid
props.description: Default sort column. Will default to the first column. If you wish to see it in effect in StoryBook, please set it and refresh the page.

props.name: sortdefaultdirection
props.description:
Default sort direction

values: `ascending` `descending`. If you wish to see it in effect in StoryBook, please set it and refresh the page.

props.name: sortdisabled
props.description: Disable sorting

props.name: sortmanual
props.description: Enables manual sorting for the table. If this is true, you will be expected to sort your data before it is passed to the table. This is useful if you are doing server-side sorting. Find an example in the 06-Sorting under Examples.

props.name: sortdisableoninitialload
props.description: Disables sorting on the initial load of the table, so the data is shown in the same order as passed via. data prop. User is still able to see sorting (arrow) buttons, and manually trigger sorting on every column.

props.name: pagination
props.description: Enable integrated pagination controls below the table. When enabled, pagination will be automatically managed. For completed story example please go to Stories Examples.

props.name: paginationmode
props.description:
Pagination mode (static or dynamic). Use `static` when you pass the whole dataset to the table, and want to use client-side pagination built into the component.
Use `dynamic` when you want to get data set from the server, and call your rest API on each `pagechange` event.

props.name: paginationtotalrecords
props.description: Total number of items / rows in your data or results. The number of total pages will be automatically calculated based on that number.

props.name: paginationarialabel
props.description: The `aria-label` for the pagination nav wrapper inside the component.

props.name: paginationcurrentpage
props.description: Number of the selected page. Always manually set to 1 or higher when pagination is enabled.

props.name: paginationhidepagesize
props.description: If set to `true`, the select with options to pick number of items per page is hidden.

props.name: paginationpagesize
props.description: The number of items to display per page. Based on this value and the `totalrecords` prop, the total number of pages will be calculated automatically. This is also the value that will be emitted in the `pagesizechange` event, and what is set as value in the page size select.

props.name: paginationpagesizeoptions
props.description: Page size options, that have to be passed as an array of numbers.

props.name: paginationhidepagestatus
props.description: If set to `true`, hides the status text showing the current range of rows. For example: "1-10 of 80 rows".

props.name: paginationshowfirstandlastpage
props.description: If set to `true`, displays first and last navigation buttons (double chevrons).

props.name: paginationscrolltotop
props.description: Table will scroll to top left after page change.

props.name: paginationloading
props.description: When true, will show a loading indicator overlay on the table.

props.name: paginationalignment
props.description: The alignment of the pagination. Is applied to only when page size and page status are hidden.

props.name: select
props.description: Show a row selector checkbox column

props.name: selectalllabel
props.description: Label for "select all rows" checkbox

props.name: selectlabel
props.description: Label for "select row" checkbox

props.name: selected
props.description: Can be used to set selected rows programatically. Pass data objects that you want to select.

props.name: selectrowdisabled
props.description: Disables the selection of disabled rows

props.name: selectsticky
props.description: Make the row selector column "sticky"

props.name: expand
props.description: When true, will enable row expansion for provided slots, check the 07-Expansion stories under Example for more.

props.name: expandopened
props.description: Can be used to set expanded rows programmatically. Pass data objects that you want to select.

props.name: expandpadding
props.description: Padding for expanded rows. `default` will apply the default padding, `none` will remove padding.

props.name: expandtriggerlabel
props.description: Label for "expand row" button. the label is hidden and only used for screen readers.

props.name: expandsticky
props.description: Make the row expansion column "sticky"

props.name: columnresize
props.description: Enable column resizing functionality.

events.name: columnresizestart
events.description: Dispatched when user starts resizing a column.

events.name: columnresizing
events.description: Dispatched continuously (animation frame throttled) while a column is being resized.

events.name: columnresizeend
events.description: Dispatched when user ends resizing a column.

events.name: rowmouseenter
events.description: Dispatched when the cursor enters a row.

events.name: rowmouseleave
events.description: Dispatched when the cursor leaves a row.

events.name: hidecolumnschange
events.description: Dispatched when a column is hid/shown with the current hidecolumns state as detail.

events.name: rowclick
events.description: Dispatched when a row is clicked.

events.name: sortchange
events.description: Dispatched when sorting direction on a column changes with a column id and direction as detail.

events.name: selectchange
events.description: Dispatched when a row is selected/deselected with the current selection state as detail.

events.name: expandchange
events.description: Dispatched when a row is expanded/collapsed with the current expansion state as detail.

events.name: paginationpagechange
events.description: Dispatched when the page changes (only when pagination is enabled) with the visible rows as detail.

events.name: paginationpagesizechange
events.description: Dispatched when the page size changes.

events.name: rowselected
events.description: Dispatched when a row is selected.

events.name: rowdeselected
events.description: Dispatched when a row is deselected.

events.name: rowexpanded
events.description: Dispatched when a row is expanded.

events.name: rowcollapsed
events.description: Dispatched when a row is collapsed.

slots.description:
Use the {rowId}_{columnId} named slot, when you want to pass other mc-components or your own custom components to the table cell.
The slot is meant to be used together with a loop function within mc-table tag (using i.e. array.map, v-for, *ngFor). See the Using slots story for implementation details and code examples.
The first parameter in the slot name {rowId} is a row id(the component will automatically try to access an `id` attribute, you can change that by passing the `datakey` attribute), the second {columnId} is the id of the column where custom component should be rendered, i.e. 10_status, 11_status, 12_status, etc. Check stories under Examples folder to see live example.
slots.name: {rowId}_{columnId}

slots.description: Use the caption named slot, A slot for a caption for the table.. Check stories under Examples folder to see live example.
slots.name: caption

slots.description: Use the {columnId}_header named slot, Header slot for a column with {columnId} as an `id` property.. Check stories under Examples folder to see live example.
slots.name: {columnId}_header

slots.description: Use the {columnId}_footer named slot, Footer slot for a column with {columnId} as an `id` property.. Check stories under Examples folder to see live example.
slots.name: {columnId}_footer

slots.description: Use the {rowId}_expanded named slot, Slot for an expandable row content.. Check stories under Examples folder to see live example.
slots.name: {rowId}_expanded

slots.description: Use the state named slot, Slot for displaying the state of the table, i.e. loading, empty, error, etc.. Check stories under Examples folder to see live example.
slots.name: state

types.name: CellTemplateDetails

members.name: value
members.description: The raw value of the cell (e.g., status: 'On schedule')

members.name: rowData
members.description: The entire row data object containing all fields for that row

members.name: column
members.description: The column configuration (id, label, width, etc.)

members.name: html
members.description: Lit's html template tag function for creating HTML templates with dynamic values

types.name: HeaderTemplateDetails

members.name: column
members.description: The column configuration (id, label, width, etc.)

members.name: html
members.description: Lit's html template tag function for creating HTML templates with dynamic values

members.name: labels
members.description:
Labels object for customizing component text.
If not provided, labels are automatically loaded based on document's lang attribute.

IMcTableLabels

Labels configuration for mc-table component
Extends pagination labels (which includes common labels) with table-specific labels

cssParts.description: Using this CSS part you can change the look of the container element inside the mc-pagination component where the main page navigation is located (buttons, select or input). Check Examples -> Customised appearance story to see live example.
cssParts.name: pagination-navigation

cssParts.description: Using this CSS part you can change the look of the container where page size select is located (if enabled). Check Examples -> Customised appearance story to see live example.
cssParts.name: pagination-pagesize

cssParts.description: Using this CSS part you can change the look of the container where page status text is located (if enabled). Check Examples -> Customised appearance story to see live example.

guidelines.title: Table
guidelines.description: Displays data in a grid of rows and columns allowing users to scan, analyse, compare, and act upon the information within.

best-practice: Use to display supplementary information under a table row.
Expanded content should be organised and easy to read and scan.
Use headings, bullet points, or tables to present the information in a structured way.
Use a side panel or <PageLink path="/components/modal">modal</PageLink> in cases where the content takes up a lot of space.
Maintain context by allowing the user to see the original row and its contents
Keep row expansion to 1 level - further levels will increase the chances of information overload.
implementations: The Maersk Design System offers two table options for developers:

1. Table component located in the @maersk/mds-components-core package. All examples on this page use this component. This supercedes the community developed web component located in the @maersk/mds-components-community package.

2. CSS classes for constructing your own HTML tables. These can be found in our CSS foundations package @maersk/mds-foundations
headers-footers: Table headers communicate what data is being presented within each column and/or row and table footers are useful when summarisation of the displayed data is needed.
headers: Most often table headers are positioned at the top row of a table. However, in some cases, it can be appropriate to 'flip' a table by using its first column to display headers. In rare cases a table may even need to have multiple headers to adequately describe the data, as in this example:
header-tooltips: When a header needs additional explanation in order to assist the user, we recommend using the <PageLink path="/components/tooltip/">tooltip component</PageLink>. When a column description is provided to the MDS table component, this pattern is automatically applied. Only use tooltips when the labels might be misunderstood or consist of acronyms (e.g. INCO terms).
header-groups: Group headers can help avoid duplication of text labels in the headers and keep the overall table cleaner and easier to scan and read.
footers: The bottom row can be used as a table footer which is especially useful when numerical data has to be summarised. The footer should clearly state that it represents a summary and be visually distinct from the other rows so that it is clear to the user that its data is different.
sorting-and-filtering: Alphabetically / numerically in ascending order (↑ 0-9, a-z)
Alphabetically / numerically in descending order (↓ 9-0, z-a)
content: Tables present a concise overview of relevant data to make a decision or draw an insight. It is best to apply "less is more" approach to selecting and structuring the data in order to conserve space, reduce visual clutter, and avoid information overload.
declutter-the-data: Consider replacing repetitive data entries with conventional (commonly understood) icons, e.g. checkmarks and crosses instead of "Yes" and "No". Common units can be abbreviated and displayed in the column header instead of being repeated in every cell. Table headers should be kept as short as possible to increase clarity and avoid truncation or wrapping.
alignment: The table content can be aligned both horizontally and vertically. Applying the right alignment of the content makes it significantly easier to consume and understand the data.
horizontal-alignment: Left-align text (as well as non-countable numerals such as dates and phone numbers)
vertical-alignment: By default, each cell's content is vertically aligned to the middle but can be changed to top or bottom-aligned. Which alignment to choose depends on how much variation there exists between the content of the cells and the row height.
middle-aligned-centered: Use vertical middle alignment when the content in a row only varies slightly up to 3 lines.
top-aligned: Use top alignment when the content in a row varies more than 3-4 lines.
bottom-aligned: In most cases, it doesn’t make sense to align cell content vertically to the bottom. When we align to the bottom we may risk that the content is hidden away e.g. by being chopped by the browser viewport. This is especially true when we are dealing with multi-line cell content and when there is a bigger variation in the length of the content between the cells within the row.
colspan-and-rowspan: Sometimes you might want to merge multiple cells across rows or columns. In such cases, you use the colspan and rowspan. For example, you might need a data description that spans over multiple columns or a data label that spans multiple rows.
example-of-rowspan: Both colspan and rowspan help improve table structure, readability, and presentation, making them essential for a well-formatted HTML table.
missing-cell-content: Display a dash (-) when a cell contains no data to let the user know that the content was loaded.
cell-content-overflow: If a cell contains too much data to be displayed in a single line the text should wrap (increasing the height of the entire row). In certain cases it may be desirable to truncate the text instead – but this comes with the risk of hiding valuable information from the user and requires implementing an alternative means for the user to access the full text string (e.g. a <PageLink path="/components/tooltip">tooltip</PageLink>). Read more in the <PageLink path="/content/truncation">truncation guidelines</PageLink>.
large-data-sets: Tables with large set of data can be difficult to display in the available screen space.

The main risk of showing tables with overflow (due to data abundance and/or screen size) is that the user loses their overview. One way of mitigating this problem is to make the header row and/or first column sticky, thus allowing the user to view the context of the data anywhere in the table.
horizontal-scroll: When the table columns can not fit in the available screen space, allow the user to scroll horizontally to see the excess columns. It is simple, scalable and conventional solution. When the table has too many columns all potential solutions to handle them have drawbacks.
importance-first: Arrange columns in order of importance, typically left to right. This will allow the user to quickly scan and compare the most important attributes of the data entries. This is especially valuable on narrow screens where the right-most columns will be hidden from the view port.
An exception to this rule of thumb is when two or more columns of unequal importance has a tight logical connection, e.g. ‘departure time’ and ‘arrival time’.
combine-columns-to-conserve-space: To save horizontal space some columns with closely related data can be combined by adding the second attribute as a subtext below the first.
expandable-rows: Use expandable rows to add and display simple supplementary information to a table row.
complex-supplementary-information: When the supplementary information to a table row requires complex interactions or is large in volume it is better to consider using alternative patterns to disclose it. For example, a side panel or a <PageLink path="/components/modal">modal</PageLink> to view and interact with the information are good alternatives. It will help shift the user focus from the table content to the complex supplementary information when it is required. This will help reduce cognitive load while maintaining the context.
expanding-multiple-rows-at-the-same-time: Consider how large is the content of each expanded row and if it takes up more than 50% of the available screen space. If that’s the case, avoiding using batch expansion as a pattern. Consider opting for a modal or aside panel to disclose the supplementary information.
pagination​: When there are too many rows for the user to get an overview of on a single page, split the rows up into pages and allow the user to navigate between them with a <PageLink path="/components/pagination">pagination</PageLink> control. Be aware that there are <PageLink path="/components/pagination#pagination-alternatives">alternative ways</PageLink> of splitting rows into consumable chunks and if you are dealing with a substantial amount of rows (i.e. more than 200) a more advanced pagination control might be necessary. Depending on the context, the pagination can be placed above, below or both above and below the table.
states: When showing different states of the table it is recommended to keep the table headers visible so the user knows what data is expected to be loaded in the context.
empty-state: Consider using an empty state when the table needs to be populated either manually or by an external source (e.g. file import).
loading: When data takes more than 1 second to load show a loading state for the table to let the user know content is being loaded. The more accurate information about the loading and progress you can display the better.
no-results-state: When data is not available either due to too narrow search and filtering criteria or another reason, make sure to communicate the reason and provide a way for the user to recover from the dead end.
error-and-warning-state: In cases where there is an API problem or data can’t be loaded from the server, it should be clearly communicated via the <PageLink path="/components/notification/">Notification component</PageLink> that can be shown instead of the table rows.
styling-options: Visual dividers and other forms of table decoration should be kept at a minimum in order to make the table less busy and easier to digest.
grid-lines: **Horizontal** grid lines visually separate the rows from each other and help the user scan along a row from start to finish. These are the most basic form of table decoration and should be applied by default since they serve as useful guides for reading the table.

**Vertical** grid lines visually separate the columns from each other and help the user compare the same data across multiple rows. However, for the most part these are not necessary if the data is <PageLink path="/components/table/#cell-content-alignment">strictly aligned</PageLink>, because the alignment itself serves as a visual separation. Whereas avoiding a spreadsheet-like expression is desirable for basic data tables it is conventional and serves a purpose for large/advanced data grids.
row-highlight-on-hover: Another way of distinguishing rows from one another is to highlight a single one with a slightly different background colour when it is hovered by the user’s mouse cursor. This way the user gets to focus in on a single row of interest at a time without being overwhelmed by other elements of table decoration. This approach comes without the risk of table clutter but is unfortunately not useful on touch devices.
zebra-stripes: If a table is exceptionally wide it can be useful to allow the user to distinguish between rows be giving every other row a slightly different background colour. However, this adds to the visual clutter and might cause contrast issues or visually collide with other elements of the table. So before applying this style to a table, consider the alternatives for row distinction (e.g. horizontal grid lines, row highlight on hover) and carefully weigh the pro against the cons.
best-practices: Provide a caption (or alt text) for the table
Avoid using tables for layout purposes
If the table contains (many) interactive elements use a grid instead

designPrinciples.title: Spacing
designPrinciples.description: Consistent spacing creates groups, rhythm and good scan-ability.

use-4-pixel-spacing: The base unit for spacing is 4 pixels, subsequent units are multiples of 4 (such as 8, 12, 16, etc.). These units should be used consistently for all paddings, margins, gutters, typography, and layouts to ensure a cohesive appearance and user experience across all Maersk digital products.
exceptions-to-4-pixel-spacing: When creating components, finer control is needed for spacing elements. Three acceptable exceptions here are 2, 6, and 10 pixels.
components: Relate the component elements and still keep them distinct by using space units dervided from the 4-pixel base spacing. These space units will make aligning and using non-core components with core ones easier.
patterns: A recognisable pattern is created when consistent spacing is used to establish a visual rhythm and relationship between the pattern elements.

Pattern spacing needs to be coherent which means not all space units need to be the same. Several spacing units can help establish better visual balance when dealing with differently-sized UI elements in the pattern.
layouts: Visual hierarchy in layouts requires well spaced regions and UI elements. Spacing units can control the information density of any application layout. Use them to provide the right level for the context and users' expectation.
information-density: Space is the element that controls the information density of any interface. Adjusting the space between blocks on information creates a visual rhythm and hierarchy.

High information density can be disorienting and overwhelming if spacing does not allow the eyes to rest and let people process what they see. People may notice the grouped elements but not process each individual item.

We recommend reading the <PageLink path="/design-language/component-fit/">Fit guidelines</PageLink> to help you make an informed decision what spacing units will be most suitable for your layout composition.

relatedGuidelines.title: Accessibility
relatedGuidelines.description: Improving the user experience for everyone.

what-is-accessibility?: Accessibility means that websites, tools and technologies are designed and developed in a way that makes it possible for people with disabilities to use them. Accessibility also benefits people without disabilities for example people with temporary disabilities or situational disabilities.
Accessibility helps people perceive, understand and interact with our digital products and it takes auditory, cognitive, neurological, physical, speech and visual limitations into consideration.

But accessibility is also a mindset and a change in the way we think and talk about digital products. It is a cultural change that cannot be made by one team only. We, therefore, urge all our colleagues as well as top management to get involved and share awareness about this very important topic. We need your support!
why-is-accessibility-important?: One of the main myths about accessibility is that it only concerns a small group of people with severe disabilities. This assumption is far from the reality and according to the World Health Organisation (WHO), more than 1 billion people are living with some level of impairment that affects the way they interact with digital products and access information online.
When our websites, apps and other digital products are designed badly, they create barriers that exclude people and make access to our offerings cumbersome and in some cases not possible at all.
being-inaccessible-is-a-problem: It is an ethical problem when we exclude certain groups of people from our products and services.

It is a legal problem, especially in the US where companies are being sued if they are not accessible, which could have financial consequences in terms of fines as well as affecting the brand negatively.

It is a big business problem - if we choose to exclude a very big group of potential customers because they have a disability.
When we design and build products with accessibility in mind, we not only open up our products and experiences for more people, but our products start to become more human - in the sense that they reflect the diversity of society and become much more flexible and robust to accommodate many different needs and use cases.

relatedGuidelines.title: "Search, filter and sort"
relatedGuidelines.description: Help users narrow down data and find what they need.

search: Use search concepts to find specific information within a large dataset. Search can support users when they don't know exactly what they are looking for and need to explore the dataset by entering keywords or phrases. Search concepts usually are combined with filter and/or sorting to help refine any results further.
Read more about <PageLink path="/guidelines/search-filter-and-sort/search-patterns/">search best practices and patterns</PageLink>.
filter: Use filter concepts to narrow down a dataset based on specific criteria. Filters help people to show only the items that meet their criteria such as a range of values, a category, or a certain date range. Filters are often used when people have a general idea of what they are looking for but want to narrow down the options to a manageable set.
Read more about <PageLink path="/guidelines/search-filter-and-sort/filter-patterns/">filter best practices and patterns</PageLink>.
sort: Use sort concepts to organise data based on certain criteria. Sorting allows users to quickly identify patterns or trends within the dataset. Sorting is often used when users have a large dataset and want to view the information in a more structured way such as alphabetical, numerical or chronological order.
Read more about <PageLink path="/guidelines/search-filter-and-sort/sort-patterns/">sort best practices and patterns</PageLink>.
